event_blocks.summary: The video commences with a black screen and ethereal sound, transitioning to the Netflix logo, followed by 'A NETFLIX ORIGINAL DOCUMENTARY SERIES' superimposed on an aerial view of a bustling New York City cityscape. A percussive beat begins as the scene shifts to ground level, showing pedestrians on a crowded sidewalk with 'NEW YORK CITY' text overlaid. A female voice observes, "I walk outside and I see typography everywhere," as the camera pans across storefront signs like 'STONE & THINGS' and '220 FIFTH'. She continues, "New York City is a city of signs," describing them as "Sometimes things written by hand, mismatched, hung up in some peculiar way," and expressing a desire to "readjust that sign, that's just absolutely awful." A close-up of a yellow 'NO BILLS' sign is shown, followed by a blonde woman walking down the sidewalk. The voice notes, "The way numbers are on doors. No two the same down the block. All messages are different, and they're everywhere." As the background music builds, she declares, "Typography is painting with words," and passionately adds, "That's my biggest high. It's my crack." The video then transitions to a vibrant title card featuring 'THE ART OF DESIGN' and 'PAULA SCHER' against a rainbow background. The segment opens with a fast-paced montage of graphic designs and text, including theatrical promotions like 'BRING IN 'DA NOISE, BRING IN 'DA FUNK' and 'HAMLET,' and stylized posters for plays like 'HAIR.' As a female voice states, "Paula Scher is the goddess of graphic design. Her stuff is everywhere," the visuals shift to a collage of album covers and pop culture references, followed by a 'MORGAN NEVILLE' title card. A male voice comments, "Paula's been able to come up with more ways to make type talk than anyone else, and to create a distinctive body of work just with letters," as the sequence displays a grid of logos from brands like Citi and Tiffany & Co. Executive producer credits appear in red text on black, followed by a stylized 'PAULA SCHER' title card with a pop-art portrait. The segment concludes with abstract geometric patterns and the words 'SKELETON CREW,' before transitioning to an exterior shot of a building covered in large typography, including 'THEATER,' 'MUSIC,' and 'POETRY,' and finally a red wall with 'EDUCATION = FREEDOM' in white text, all underscored by continuous energetic music.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative commences with a dynamic title sequence featuring bold red and white text, the Netflix logo, and executive producer credits against geometric red and teal accents, accompanied by upbeat electronic music. This transitions to a vibrant mosaic of brand logos and a red screen displaying the director's credit, 'Directed by Richard Press.' As the music fades, a blonde woman, identified as Paula Scher and wearing a red scarf, black vest, and white shirt, enters a modern, open-plan office, her voice reflecting, "But when I go to work every day, I feel like I'm navigating myself through a maze." The camera captures her moving through the office, greeting colleagues, and sketching at a desk before reviewing a floor plan on a computer screen. The text 'PENTAGRAM DESIGN NEW YORK' identifies the location, and Scher, described as a Pentagram Partner, discusses her work, stating, "I sit nose to nose with my partners. My team is on the fourth floor, I have to run up and down steps to see them," and mentions "solving things on scraps of paper," and "starting something, getting interrupted." The office environment, filled with design elements and natural light, buzzes with ambient sounds. Scher further describes Pentagram as a "design cooperative" with "no boss, just friends," and contrasts her process with traditional office work, explaining she "could never walk into an office and sit down at my desk to design," as she'd "accomplish nothing." Instead, she clears her desk, reads emails, and sorts through "junk mail" to make "a little order," before confronting a problem, asking "how am I going to solve this problem." Michael Bierut, also a Pentagram Partner, calls Scher an "indispensable player" within the "super group" of designers. The segment concludes with Scher applying lipstick in a mirror, suggesting her method of finding clarity: "Then you walk up the stairs, go into the ladies' room, put on your lipstick and figure it out."

event_blocks.summary: In a modern design studio, designer Kirstin Huber, wearing a teal top and colorful headband, works on a computer displaying a bold, comic-style typographic design for 'GIRLS! BOYS!' with Shakespearean references. The Blonde Woman, in a red scarf and black vest, reviews the digital design, expressing approval with "Oh, I love it like that. It's very slurpy," and elaborating on the benefits of collaborative ideation: "Ideas can be triggered by working with my team... I see more than I would see if I was just doing it all by myself. That's fantastic." She provides specific feedback on the 'GIRLS! BOYS!' design, instructing Kirstin to "make girls and boys the same way with the exception of the, you know, pointy things. And then figure out how the other type intersects and then we got to figure out color." While speaking, the Blonde Woman transitions from digital review to sketching the concept by hand in a notebook with a black pen, her precise hand movements and rings highlighted in close-ups. She reveals the project context: "We're working on the Summer Festival posters for the Public Theater. They put on free Shakespeare in Central Park every summer," and discusses the 'Taming of the Shrew' element. After Kirstin's agreement, the Blonde Woman encourages, "give it a shot." The scene then shifts to a nighttime urban setting featuring a theater marquee with a 'Macbeth' poster, accompanied by upbeat, driving electronic music, reinforcing the Public Theater connection. The Blonde Woman's voice continues over the music, stating, "I've been designing for the Public Theater..." The Netflix logo is visible throughout.

event_blocks.summary: In 1994, Paula Scher embarked on her inaugural project: creating an identity for the Public Theater, a task complicated by its multiple existing designations, including the Joseph Papp Public Theater and Shakespeare in Central Park. Scher aimed to unify these identities, envisioning a "populous" and "New Yorkish" institution that was "loud and proud." Her process involved exploring typographic designs, particularly from American Wood Type, appreciating its power and diverse forms, and she meticulously examined variations of the letter 'R' and the construction of letters like 'E', noting how subtle adjustments to elements like the midline and cap height could evoke different eras and feelings. Scher explained that typography possesses immense power, conveying character, weight, and height, and that designers work with elements that define these attributes. She demonstrated this by manipulating the letter 'E', showing how altering the length of its middle bar could make it appear either 1930s-inspired or modern. This exploration of typographic principles was visually reinforced through a montage of vibrant theatrical posters for productions such as 'Hamilton,' 'The Tempest,' and 'Hair,' alongside a typographic building facade. A male voice commented on Scher's ability to create instantly recognizable identities, describing her work as something that "pulled people in" and could be "dissected, taken apart, and put back together," finding it "really thrilling." The narrative also touched upon the design of the 'HIGH LINE' logo, where the objective was to evoke a railroad track rather than a simple 'H', visually represented by a comparison between actual railway tracks with greenery and a stylized 'H' transforming into a hashtag, with further explanation on how industrial elements like horizontal bars on track lines alter the overall spirit. The segment concluded with a close-up of a 'Toulouse Lautrec' poster and a technical diagram of the letter 'E' with typographic guidelines, underscoring the craftsmanship involved in design.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a dynamic cityscape, showcasing a bustling urban street with storefronts and a Ryder truck, accompanied by a light, investigative background music. A female voice, later identified as Paula Scher, states, "It's 1994. My first project was creating an identity for the theater." The scene transitions to a close-up of a building's ornate facade with a 'JOE'S PUB' sign, then shifts to a historical black-and-white montage of the Public Theater, including archival footage of outdoor Shakespeare performances and a sign for the New York Shakespeare Festival. Scher continues, "When they hired me, they had a name issue." An unidentified male voice elaborates on the challenge, explaining, "One of the things at the time that was very challenging about the Public Theater, it had these multiple identities. It was the Public Theater. And then some people called it the Joseph Papp Public Theater. And then there was Shakespeare in Central Park. I wanted everything to feel like it was of one, that it was breathing fully as an institution." The visuals continue to show the historic Public Theater and its outdoor stages. Scher's voice returns, emphasizing the desired identity: "It had to be populous. I knew it had to be New Yorkish, meaning it had to be loud and proud." The narrative then moves to a modern interior of the theater, highlighting its vibrant lobby with colorful posters and signage for 'Anspacher-Martinson' and 'LuEsther-Shiva' theaters. The focus shifts to Paula Scher, a woman with blonde hair, in an archive room filled with boxes. She is seen flipping through a book of typographic designs, with distinct sounds of pages turning. She explains, "I was flipping through one of my favorite books, American Wood Type. I like American Wood Type because it's powerful and it has many forms." The segment concludes with a close-up of her hand turning pages, revealing detailed typography, including multiple variations of the letter 'R', as she notes, "On this particular page were these Rs, and they go back to the skinniest form or..." The Netflix logo remains in the top right corner throughout the live-action scenes.

The video opens with a close-up of hands, adorned with rings including a turquoise stone, flipping through a book of typographic designs featuring bold, stylized letterforms like multiple 'R's and 'E's against a red background, with the Netflix logo visible. A female voice, identified as Paula Scher, explains her process of making the word "public" in the widest form, including every type of weight to symbolize all of New York, and how one can create an identity for a whole place based on the recognizability of type. The scene transitions to Paula Scher herself, seated at a table in an archive room filled with shelves of labeled cardboard boxes, reviewing design materials including a yellow folder labeled 'PUBLIC' and a book of typographic samples. Upbeat, rhythmic music begins as the camera cuts to a montage of vibrant, dynamic theatrical posters for productions such as 'Hamilton,' 'The Tempest,' and 'Love's Labor's Lost,' showcasing bold typography and striking color contrasts. A male voice comments that Paula's work "pulled people in," and you instantly knew "the Public," describing the language as something that could be dissected, taken apart, and put back together, finding it "really thrilling." The montage continues, including a detailed breakdown of typographic elements like cap height, midline, and baseline, using a large white 'E' on a red background. Paula Scher's voice returns, stating that "typography can create immense power," explaining that designers work with things that create character, weight, and height. The montage rapidly displays more posters for 'Winter’s Solitude,' 'WARS,' and 'Hair,' each with unique typography. Paula then uses the example of taking an 'E' where the middle bar is the same length as the ends, noting how it feels different. The video concludes with a close-up of a 'Toulouse Lautrec' poster in bold red letters on a pink background, followed by a technical diagram of a white 'E' with typographic guidelines, reinforcing the theme of design craftsmanship.

The video opens with a typographic diagram on a red background, illustrating the letter 'E' with labeled cap height, midline, and baseline, as a male voice explains how adjusting the bar's length can make a typeface appear like it was drawn in the 1930s or more modern. This explanation continues as the scene rapidly transitions through a vibrant, chaotic collage of comic-style text like 'KABOOM!' and 'POW!', a theatrical setting for 'Il Barbiere di Siviglia', a blonde woman speaking directly to the camera, a colorful graphic of Japan featuring a sumo wrestler and 'SUPER HAPPY FUN FACTS!!', and a dynamic collage of the word 'Blah'. The male voice elaborates on how font weight and style, whether heavy and bold or thin and serif, convey feelings of immediacy or classicism, concluding that typography communicates "sensibility and spirit" that becomes "spectacular" when combined with meaning. Light, rhythmic, slightly jazzy background music then begins. The scene shifts to a series of theatrical posters for productions like 'Hamilton' and 'The Tempest', followed by a typographic building facade. The male voice returns, explaining that when he designed the 'HIGH LINE' logo, the goal was to make it resemble a railroad track more than an 'H'. This is visually demonstrated by a split-screen comparison: actual railway tracks overgrown with greenery on the left, and a stylized 'H' evolving into a hashtag on a red background on the right. The music continues as the male voice further details how adding horizontal bars to a railroad track line can make it appear "fairly industrial" and "totally changes the spirit," while a wide view of the High Line park shows people walking alongside the rusted tracks, with red lines highlighting the structure.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a split-screen showcasing a 'HIGH LINE' sign and pedestrians in an urban park, transitioning to an aerial view of Salisbury, Connecticut, at dusk. In a sunlit studio, Paula Scher, a blonde woman in a gray top and black apron, approaches a large, colorful mural, rustling paper sounds accompanying her as she begins detailed brushwork. Scher reflects on her past practice of hand-painting fonts and her subsequent disengagement from physical art-making after the late 90s computerization, stating, "The physical loss was huge for me, and that's why I started painting." She explains her deliberate choice to paint maps, finding satisfaction in their rote execution, and identifies a section as "Utah." The scene then shifts to a close-up of a hand pointing to a vibrant, textured map of the United States, where a female voice explains the dotted lines represent distances and the background is zip codes, emphasizing the map's emotional rather than factual nature. As the camera pans across the map, filled with typographic elements and colorful state representations, the voice notes Wyoming's sparse population but palpable presence. Contemplative string music plays as the view widens to reveal the entire map on a large canvas in the bright studio, with Scher holding it up. She clarifies the painting depicts "counties and zip codes" and is designed to raise questions rather than answer them, posing queries about county distribution relative to state size. A thump is heard as she adjusts the canvas. Close-ups highlight the map's intricate detail in regions like the Midwest and Northeast, featuring city names like 'Chicago,' 'Detroit,' and 'New York City.' Scher identifies it as a "demographic map" detailing "average age of people, racial and ethnic breakdowns," stressing the need to "sit and read it" to comprehend its "equivalently complicated and ridiculous" information. The video concludes with a split-screen of the map's eastern and western sections, underscoring its scope, with the Netflix logo consistently visible.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a split-screen view, showing a gray 'HIGH LINE' sign with a white maple leaf logo under an overpass on the left, while on the right, people stroll through a modern urban park with brick and glass buildings. This transitions to a serene aerial view of Salisbury, Connecticut, at dusk, with bare trees and a calm lake under a cloudy sky, overlaid with the text 'SALISBURY CONNECTICUT'. Footsteps echo as the scene shifts to a bright, sunlit studio where Paula Scher, a woman with blonde hair wearing a gray long-sleeve top and a black apron, walks toward a large, colorful mural on the wall. Sounds of paper rustling accompany her as she approaches a ladder and begins painting with a fine brush, focusing intently on adding intricate details to the mural. Paula explains, "I used to paint my fonts by hand when I was a young designer... and I really miss it." She continues, "When we became fully computerized in the late 90s, I didn't touch anything and I didn't use my hands. In the past, I cut things up, I ripped things, I pasted things. I touched art supplies." Close-ups reveal her hand carefully applying red paint to white rectangular shapes on the mural, which features a vibrant, geometric map-like design filled with names like 'BINGHAMTON' and 'DELHI' in bold yellow letters, along with numbers. Paula adds, "The physical loss was huge for me, and that's why I started painting." She pauses to speak, gesturing toward the artwork, identifying a section with "Uh-oh. That's Utah," then resumes painting, dipping her brush into a red paint container. She concludes, "I deliberately began painting the maps because they would take me a long time to accomplish in some very rote way." The studio is well-lit with natural light, and various art supplies are visible, with the Netflix logo consistently in the top right corner.

The video opens with a close-up of a hand, adorned with a gold ring, pointing to a vibrant, textured map of the United States. A female voice explains that "These dotted lines are the distances between two given points" and "the background is the zip codes," adding that the artwork is "not factual, it's emotional." As the camera pans across the map, revealing intricate patterns and colorful sections representing different states filled with typographic elements, the voice continues, noting that "Wyoming doesn't have very many people in it," but "you feel it instead of know it." Gentle, contemplative string music begins to play as the scene transitions to a wider view of the entire map, now displayed on a large canvas. The woman with blonde hair, wearing a gray top and black apron, stands in a bright, sunlit art studio, holding up the massive, colorful, typographic artwork. She clarifies, "I'm not making something designed to answer questions, it's more designed to raise them," further explaining that "This painting is counties and zip codes." She poses a question: "Why do some little states have a million counties and some big states have very few?" A distinct thump sound is heard as she adjusts the canvas. The camera zooms in on various regions, highlighting the complexity and density of the design, such as the Midwest and the Northeast, where city names like 'Chicago,' 'Detroit,' and 'New York City' are prominently featured. The artist then identifies it as a "demographic map," detailing its content: "average age of people, racial and ethnic breakdowns." She emphasizes that "to actually have any sense of it, you have to actually sit and read it," concluding that "the information is equivalently complicated and ridiculous." The video concludes with a split-screen view of the map’s eastern and western sections, emphasizing its vast scope and artistic depth, with the Netflix logo visible throughout.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with an upbeat musical score accompanying a pan across a densely packed typographic world map featuring the Netflix logo and regions like 'MANHATTAN' and 'ABU DHABI', transitioning to artist Paula Scher, with blonde hair and a gray top and black apron, painting a large, colorful mural resembling a stylized map with city names like 'BINGHAMTON' and 'DELHI'. Paula explains, "I used to make complicated, nonsensical charts and diagrams that were satirical," as the video cuts to a hand-drawn map of the United States filled with handwritten text and political satire. She describes her work as "silly information, fractured information," done "to make points," while a chaotic, text-heavy collage with headlines like 'TERRORISTS' and 'MARC RICH' appears. Paula adds, "Then I started charting things that were not chartable," often "mostly denigrating my own physical appearance," as a vintage-style poster titled 'The many styles of PAULA SCHER' is shown. Paula finds her work "funny," noting that "sometimes they're pithy and more meaningful," like the dense typewritten document filled with personal and financial data, which she describes as "all my numbers on my credit cards, just to show how many numbers were attached to my name." A bold, hand-drawn title 'FAMILY' appears, followed by a close-up of a hand-drawn calendar titled 'A 28 DAY CYCLE' detailing daily moods. Paula explains that her works "later they became political or nonsensical," and "ultimately turned into my paintings," as the scene returns to Paula painting the mural, carefully applying red paint, and she comments, "Oh, that one has a period. Maybe they should have periods." The scene then shows Paula intently painting the mural, reflecting on the "obsessive quality" of her work as "the act of weaving little bits of information to make a bigger thing," accompanied by light, reflective background music. A male voice briefly comments, "Okay, that's definitely stronger than that," before Paula states her journey "started when I was very young," recounting a "very high IQ score in something called quantitative reasoning," which her family expected to be "math," but was instead "the ability to synthesize a lot of information and come to a conclusion." The scene transitions to a black-and-white archival photograph of a young girl (her younger self) and then to Paula in a present-day interview holding the framed photo, calling her work "a self-portrait" and revealing she was "happiest when I was making things." The narrative shifts to a surreal, close-up of the young girl’s face, overlaid with colorful, handwritten text detailing personal milestones from 1955 to 1971, such as '1955 - MINOR DYSLEXIA DISCOVERED' and '1964 - FIRST DIARY'. Over this dynamic visual, Paula rapidly narrates her past, mentioning "1956 orthopedic shoes," "1959 developed a contempt for girl scouts," and "1953 discovered I'm Jewish," then lists "every hairdo I ever had," including "the blunt cut, the Sassoon, the shag, the summer blonde, the platinum blonde, the street blonde, the reddish blonde, and no blonde," as the text culminates in 'THE TRUTH BEHIND THE BLONDE' and 'I AM PAULA SCHER'. She concludes by stating she "didn't really fit in very well at high school," preferring to go to "art classes instead of going to the football game," as the final shot shows a vintage color photograph of a young blonde woman. The video ends with a close-up of Paula's focused face as she works, with the Netflix logo visible, and a male voice is heard asking, "So, these paintings, they seem to have..."

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a vintage color photograph of a smiling young woman against a backdrop of pinned papers and photos, accompanied by melancholic music and a male voice commenting, "you know, there's something wrong with you if you do that." This transitions to black-and-white archival footage of a stately brick building with a person reading under a tree, maintaining the serene music. A Blonde Woman, in a brightly lit interview wearing a black top and necklace, explains her unexpected love for typography while studying illustration at the Tyler School of Art, influenced by contemporary culture like Zig-Zag rolling papers, Zapp comics, underground magazines, and especially record covers. As she speaks, a vibrant, psychedelic collage of album covers and pop culture imagery, including Zig-Zag rolling papers, The Jefferson Airplane, and The Rolling Stones, appears with bold colors and dynamic graphics, shifting the background music to a lively rock/blues track with a prominent guitar riff. raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a hand, adorned with rings including a turquoise stone, sketching bold, expressive letterforms for a 'PIER 55' logo on paper. A female voice explains the design process, moving from "a more literal translation" to something that "started to abstract and become open." The scene transitions to the Blonde Woman, wearing a black top and delicate gold necklace, intently reviewing these hand-drawn sketches in her office environment, which features bookshelves and design materials. A male voice is heard commenting, "These are really good sketches." The female voice, likely the Blonde Woman, then elaborates on her work, stating, "Mostly what I design are identity systems," which "have to exist in lots and lots and lots of ways." She adds, "I generally try to want to push something as far as it can be pushed," finding "that's the fun." As rhythmic, slightly electronic background music begins, the narrative shifts to a wide shot of the Lincoln Center, with text overlays introducing 'The Metropolitan Opera', 'New York City Ballet', and 'New York Philharmonic', establishing a cultural context. The video cuts to a vibrant green title card with 'jazz' in bold white letters, followed by a smiling man with dreadlocks, identified as a musician, standing in front of a green door with the NYC Parks logo. A bustling park scene with people walking and a large 'NYC Parks' banner reinforces the theme of public space. A dynamic collage of logos, including 'BUILT', 'Parsons', 'The New School', 'truvia', 'POV', and 'Sundance Institute', highlights diverse design and cultural institutions. The Blonde Woman is then seen walking along a city sidewalk, passing a poster for 'HEAD' and a building with 'JOE'S PUB' banners, suggesting her journey through New York's cultural landscape. The female voice returns, discussing her approach to design: "I've started to trying to create a process and the identities I make where I go back and revisit them in five or ten years, because sometimes they need tweaking." She continues, "It's hard to make that a guess, and so you want to design something that can be adapted to its time," before concluding with "I've redesigned the public..." as the segment ends with a wide shot of the ornate Joe's Pub building, its grand facade, pink banners, and a passing yellow taxi creating a vibrant urban backdrop, with the background music continuing.

The segment opens with Ellen Lupton, Curator at the Cooper-Hewitt National Design Museum, in a black top and delicate gold necklace, speaking directly to the camera against a white background, explaining how she 'tightened it up, moved it apart, changed the font' of a theater logo. The scene transitions to a wide shot of the Taub Box Office at the Public Theater, where a woman in a black blazer walks past a glass display filled with vibrant theatrical posters, including 'Hamilton' and 'The Tempest,' while talking on her phone. Michael Bierut, Pentagram Partner, then appears in a suit and striped tie, seated in a well-lit office, speaking with a serious expression about his 'love affair with the Public Theater.' The video shifts to black-and-white archival footage of a dancer performing energetically on a stage, accompanied by distinct, rhythmic percussive sounds, as Bierut continues, describing how 'Paula did Bring the Noise, Bring the Funk,' which 'really signaled a paradigm shift, a new moment for the Public Theater.' A split-screen then shows a 'Bring in 'da Noise, Bring in 'da Funk' poster alongside the dancer, with Bierut explaining how Paula 'figured out a way how to take what she saw on stage and turn it into ink on paper.' The sequence continues with dynamic shots of the same poster in various formats—yellow, black-and-white, and red—emphasizing its bold typography and critical acclaim, including 'Winner of 4 1996 Tony Awards,' as Bierut describes the type as 'from top to bottom, filled with words,' and the posters as 'crazy,' 'in your face,' and 'just like New York.' The camera pans across the exterior of the Public Theater, showcasing multiple posters for the show displayed on the building’s facade. As a rhythmic, electronic music track begins, the segment concludes with a split-screen: on the left, a Playbill cover for 'Bring in 'da Noise, Bring in 'da Funk' at the Ambassador Theatre, and on the right, a man in a tuxedo speaking at a podium, likely during a Tony Awards ceremony, while Bierut's voiceover describes the show as 'Noise Funk,' which 'was everywhere,' 'aggressive,' 'urban,' 'elegant,' and 'evocative.' The Netflix logo is consistently visible in the top right corner throughout.

event_blocks.summary: A split-screen montage opens with a formally dressed crowd at a theater event, where an announcer declares the Tony Award for Best Direction of a Musical goes to 'Bring in 'da Noise, Bring in 'da Funk,' met with applause and celebratory music, while the Netflix logo remains visible. Woody Perle, a former Pentagram partner in a striped blazer and glasses, speaks outdoors, reflecting that the musical "really, really put her on the map" and was "everywhere," noting its high quality. This is interspersed with vibrant theatrical posters for 'Bring in 'da Noise, Bring in 'da Funk,' featuring bold typography and critical acclaim, alongside posters for 'Marc Salem’s Mind Games' and 'Chicago.' Upbeat, jazzy music plays as a woman in a black top, in an interview setting, expresses frustration that "everybody began imitating it," leading to "New York City ate the Public Theater's identity." A dramatic close-up shows a man in a gold shirt shouting, with a text overlay referencing 'Hoffman' and 'De Niro.' A man in a dark jacket smiles in a theater with red seats as a voice states that within "three to five years, it became the standard." The woman in the black top continues, describing how the imitation "just made her crazy" and led her to "change the theater to not make it only that kind of typography," creating "very dark posters" with serif typography as an opposite approach. She recalls showing these to George Wolfe, who joked about "a year of depressing posters" as she approached 50.

event_blocks.summary: In a dimly lit theater, Seymour Chwast, wearing glasses and a dark jacket, laughs and speaks animatedly, questioning, "I'm sure, I did I say that? She said I said that." The scene shifts to a bright kitchen where Paula, a blonde woman, chops an avocado as their dog, Meemie, runs by. Seymour, identified as Paula's husband and a graphic designer, dons a green apron and places a bowl of dark soup topped with avocado on the counter, announcing, "Okay, we're having soup. Soup with avocado." Paula interjects, "No, me me," and Seymour playfully warns, "Watch it. This is not for you." The couple shares a meal at the kitchen island, with Paula mentioning, "I put an extra country in," and Seymour joking about her telling him "actual real news" after commenting, "Things aren't going so well in Spain." Meemie walks between them, prompting Paula to call "The doggie," and Seymour to remark on her good behavior, humorously adding, "Except for me who she attacks." He claims, "The dog loves Paul and hates me," but Paula corrects him, explaining, "Meemie really likes Seymour, but she jumps at him." Seymour insists, "She hugs me," which Paula clarifies as "She hugs you," before describing, "She jumps on me," and Seymour adds, "She jumps and bites him." The conversation continues in a cozy living room with warm gestures. Later, Paula, in a black top and gold necklace, speaks to the camera against a white background, clarifying the pronunciation of 'CHWAST,' which is drawn beside her. A female voiceover begins, stating, "thought it was funny. I thought it was a funny sounding name," and Paula elaborates on the pronunciation variations. Jazzy music starts as a vintage color photo of Paula and Seymour appears, described as "Our Elizabeth Taylor and Richard Burton tale. Graphic designers love that story." A black-and-white photo of them follows, with the voiceover identifying Seymour as a "design hero" from art school, noting he was "39, 40" and she was "21." A split-screen montage shows Seymour's face, a newspaper clipping, and a cartoon bird, while the voiceover praises him as a "sensational designer." A poster for 'WORLD BUSINESS INVEST' is shown, with the voiceover noting his "very funny" and "exceedingly political" work from the late 50s. A group photo identifies 'SEYMOUR CHWAST' and 'MILTON GLASER' as founders of Pushpin Studios with Ed Sorel. A rapid sequence of graphic artworks includes a caricature of a man with a gun, a split-screen cartoon of 'UNACCEPTABLE' vs. 'ACCEPTABLE' drawing, a caricature of a man with a bow tie, and a 'GUANTANAMO' poster, described as a style combining "pop colors, wit, and intellectual thinking." The segment ends with a red anti-war poster, and the voiceover states, "where I really started to understand that type had spirit." The Netflix logo is visible throughout.

event_blocks.summary: The video opens with a display of typographic posters, including 'POSTER STUDIOS' and 'American Fried,' featuring thematic words and imagery, while an off-screen female voice explains that art "did not have to be some clean, mechanical-like thing that was simply doing its job. It could be this marvelous thing to engage with," accompanied by jazzy background music. The scene shifts to an art studio where Seymour Chwast, wearing a green apron, meticulously carves a wooden block with a gouge, his hands steady and focused, as Paula Chwast describes his ability to "wake up and always seem to be able to work." Later, Seymour and Paula are in a living room with bookshelves and a colorful mural, where an off-screen male voice remarks, "You guys are kind of historic as a couple," prompting Paula to state, "Well, that may be, but we don't really work together," and Seymour to firmly declare, "Never," with Paula elaborating, "Never, we can't collaborate." Returning to the studio, Paula, now in a gray top and black apron, paints a large, vibrant, geometric mural with a fine brush, adding red paint to white rectangular shapes, while Seymour observes, and a faint scraping sound is audible. As the camera pans across the mural, resembling a stylized map with bold text, Seymour comments, "Washington looks a little pale," and Paula responds, "Well, it'll look more vibrant when it gets a WA on it," before reiterating, "We can't, we can't collaborate," explaining, "He can't work on my stuff and I can't work on his stuff. I don't want to." The couple then returns to the living room, smiling warmly, with Seymour quipping, "That's why we're not apart," followed by Paula's light laugh. The video concludes with an exterior shot of a large, stone house with a green awning, where a black and white dog runs across the lawn, and a person walks toward the house carrying a green bag, with the Netflix logo and calm music consistently present, along with faint traffic sounds.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a domestic scene featuring a house, a person walking on the lawn, and a dog, accompanied by a casual conversation about leaving New York City and the need for inspiration. This transitions to an urban setting with posters for the 'New York City Ballet' and '2008 Winter Season,' where a woman sits by a fountain, engrossed in her phone, as the narrator reflects on designing environmental graphics in the physical world during a time when design was becoming digital. The scene moves inside a building, showing a hallway with 'DRESSING ROOM' and 'BACKSTAGE' signs, followed by a mural with protest art and slogans like 'JUSTICE' and 'ONE VOTE,' illustrating the narrator's use of large-scale lettering to create a sense of place and aid navigation. A spacious lobby with children playing near a sculpture is shown, followed by the woman walking briskly through a plaza. The narrator concludes, "Design exists beyond screens. It has an impact in real life." The video then shifts to chaotic, storm-damaged scenes with flooded streets and debris, accompanied by sounds of strong wind and crashing water, and a news ticker displaying 'THE AFTERMATH OF SANDY TODAY' and 'COMMUNITY DEALS WITH FIRE, FLOOD, PLANE CRASH & 9/11.' Ellen Lupton explains that Hurricane Sandy devastated the economy of Rockaway Beach, destroying the boardwalk, which was spray-painted with 'SANDY WINS,' and fencing off the beach. Archival footage of a vibrant boardwalk and a skeletal roller coaster structure contrasts with the destruction. Lupton, speaking to the camera, states she was hired to create an 'emotional sign system' to bring beaches like the Rockaways, which invoke memories of a bygone era, into the 21st century. The recovery is shown through new signage like 'Rockaway Beach 32nd St' and 'B86th' beside the shoreline. Lupton emphasizes that neighborhoods retained their beaches despite the boardwalk destruction, noting the unique appearance of the beach from different entry points. The final frames depict a resilient beach with surfers, a lifeguard sign, and ocean waves, as Lupton describes creating large standing posters with photographs to orient people and emotionally connect the community while functioning as directional aids.

event_blocks.summary: The 2000 U.S. presidential election ballot, featuring candidates like George W. Bush, Al Gore, and Pat Buchanan, is examined with arrows pointing to problematic punch-hole positions, as Ellen Lupton explains the "fly ballot" design. Lupton details that the ballot was split into two columns to ensure names were legible for the elderly population of Palm Beach County, a decision made with the intention of providing a service. The layout placed George Bush first in the initial column, Al Gore second, and Pat Buchanan on the opposing side. Inside a voting booth, a woman with curly hair and a pink sweater uses a blue lever to make a selection, followed by a man in a blue patterned shirt performing a similar action. Lupton clarifies the critical design flaw: voters mistakenly believed the second punch-hole directly below Bush corresponded to Al Gore, but due to staggered holes, it actually directed the vote to Pat Buchanan. The ballot is further illustrated with highlighted selections for Bush and Gore as a lever is moved, demonstrating the widespread confusion. Lupton, speaking directly to the camera, elaborates that this design error caused a substantial number of Palm Beach County residents to unintentionally cast votes for a candidate described as an "anti-Semite." The Netflix logo is consistently visible, and the setting shifts between a studio and an office, underscoring the documentary's focus on the 2000 election's ballot design issues, while Theresa Lepore appears in an interview setting without speaking.

event_blocks.summary: In a modern theater lobby with red walls and glass partitions, featuring the Netflix logo and theatrical posters for 'GIRLS! BOYS!' and 'SHREW! WAR!', Patrick Willingham, Executive Director of The Public Theater, and a woman with tattoos and a black beanie, observe a design discussion. Ellen Lupton, a blonde woman in a black top and gold necklace, uses a blue marker on a white surface, drawing dynamic lines and stating, "The horizontal read is always better." Willingham, bald and in a gray suit, agrees, "Yeah, see these are great. When you do the horizontal, it pops. I love that." Lupton continues, "this is as high as you're ever gonna get," and "It's not as high as here, but it's good." A man in a suit questions the color viability, "Will this actually play in this color?" Lupton seeks clarification, and Willingham interjects, "I mean I love it. And but I boy, you're gonna change it when you actually do it." Lupton insists, "No, I I wanted that." The man in the suit voices his primary concern, "I have to say that's the thing that I'm most concerned about. I just think it needs to be larger." Lupton explains the negotiation process, "The meeting must end here, because what will happen is a counter-rebuttal to your offer... and we'll continue on until you reach sudden death," emphasizing the need for validation, "They want proof that this is really, really gonna work," while acknowledging, "The problem is there isn't proof. It's how do people see and perceive and accept things." The man in the suit begins to speak again, "I feel like as usual once I sit..." as the segment concludes, underscoring the collaborative and critical nature of the design process.

event_blocks.summary: An upbeat, rhythmic music track accompanies a lively event crowd, with Michael Bierut, a Pentagram Partner in a navy suit and striped tie, speaking from a bright office with large windows and bookshelves, remarking on Paula Scher's "inexhaustible" 40-plus years of continuous effort and "virtuosity."
